story:
The child came back from the tidepools singing, cradling an object shaped like a giant egg. “It’s the egg of an ancient creature. I found it. I was there.” Her four-legged shadow, Kakahiaka Noe—Foggy Morning—leapt and twisted around her as the waves breathed in and out.

Up on the hill the village watched with serious eyes. “What is this nonsense? She is not like one of us. Where is her mother?” The mother stood apart, listening, smiling, her thoughts going another way. Oh, my explorer, my dancer of ideas, you with your curious eyes. You are your great-great-great-grandmother’s child—the one the old legends say slipped into the morning mists, spiraled through time to other worlds, and came back with stories so vivid some whispered they must be true. The mothers of our mothers believed.

From toddler days, the girl drew spirals at low tide—circles linked by paths—carved with driftwood and branch. The ocean washed them away, like time itself. So the mother gave her a new name: Kai o ka Manawa, Ocean of Time. Kai, for short.

At ten, Kai ran across the bay’s slick tidepools beneath the looming rock mountain, a six-hundred-foot dome shouldered with fog. Long ago—millions of years for some creatures, thousands for others—ancestors of the living moved there. Rare souls still glided along those currents. Only those whose natural gifts survived the pressures of childhood could follow the spiral and see. Kai could. Her hair streamed like dark night clouds; her feet skipped across wet sand. Her dog’s ears pricked like sharp peaks; his tail flew like a pennant; his eyes flashed.

Before dawn she had eaten dried fish and hot broth of acorn, shellfish, and herbs, then seen something flicker in the gray. “There it is again!” She set down her basket and sprang toward the mist. “Child, what is it?” her mother called, voice thinned by salt wind. In the dim light the girl became one figure, then a line of figures, then she was gone, her cloak left behind. “I must be losing my sight,” the mother murmured.

Kai chased a hulking shape on the estuary beach: taller than three or four people, with four tusks—a massive, ancient cousin of elephants, the kind some called mastodon though this one seemed older still. She followed it through the soft-edged doorway the fog made, through the spiral the sea traced. 

After a while, she returned wet and breathless, carrying an egg-shaped stone she had found along the way. She believed it was an egg from another time, perhaps even a fossil, still warm in her hands.

The village chuckled and rolled its eyes. “We must stop this oddness,” one mother said. “I don’t want my children influenced.” The mutters grew into a plan to drive away Kai and her mother.

Quietly, the mother packed clothing, dried food, and herbs. “I’m going with you, Kai,” she said at dawn. “We are not coming back.” Kai’s eyes widened, clear as tidepools. “Yes,” she whispered. “To another time and place. All will be well. I already have friends there.”

No one noticed them slip into the marine layer where sea met sand, the dog bounding, the egg-shaped bundle held close. That night they did not return. Yet on some early mornings, through the pale veil of mist, a few villagers swore they saw silhouettes on the beach—two figures and a leaping dog—dancing, playing, moving forward in a circle, like the spirals the girl once drew and the ocean forever writes and erases.
